message:
security(verify_checksum): remove trusted-owner auto-accept shortcut

Previously, when an installer download's SHA256 did not match the
checksum in checksums.yaml, verify_checksum() would fall back to a
"trusted-tool auto-accept" path for URLs under the ACFS owner
(Dicklesworthstone). The rationale was that a mismatch on an
ACFS-owned tool is most likely a stale checksum, not a real
supply-chain attack — so the code would re-download the installer
and, if the two downloads agreed, accept it with a warning.

That reasoning is unsound for a security-critical primitive:

- A consistent hash across two consecutive downloads only proves
  the CDN is serving the same bytes, not that those bytes are
  legitimate. An attacker who has compromised a release artifact
  (or inserted a malicious build) will happily return a stable
  hash on every request.
- The trusted-owner check is a weak trust anchor: anyone who can
  forge or spoof a github URL matching the pattern is already in
  a position to bypass the whole gate.
- The fallback silently downgrades the guarantee from "bytes match
  a signed/checked-in checksum" to "bytes match themselves", which
  is what users presumably did not ask for when they ran an
  installer through verify_checksum() in the first place.

Changes:

- scripts/lib/security.sh: drop the trusted-owner mismatch fallback,
  the verify_tmp scratch file it used, and the RETURN-trap cleanup
  for it. Replace the block with a comment that records the
  rationale so future hands know why the shortcut is not coming
  back. Mismatches now always surface as a hard "Security Error:
  Checksum mismatch" and fail the install.
- tests/unit/lib/test_security.bats: add
  "rejects trusted-owner mismatch without refreshed checksum" that
  stubs out acfs_download_to_file to return bytes that don't match
  the expected hash from a Dicklesworthstone-hosted URL, and stubs
  acfs_refresh_loaded_checksums_from_remote to fail. The test
  asserts verify_checksum exits non-zero, prints "Checksum
  mismatch", and does NOT print "Trusted-tool auto-accept" — i.e.
  the removed codepath is proven gone.
- scripts/generated/internal_checksums.sh: refresh the pinned
  SHA256 for scripts/lib/security.sh so
  check-manifest-drift.sh does not flag the new (hardened) contents
  as unauthorized drift.

Impact for users: installers whose checksums.yaml is genuinely
stale must be fixed by re-generating the manifest (via the existing
check-manifest-drift.sh --fix or the checksum-monitor), not by
relying on the runtime to wave the mismatch through. This is the
whole point of having checksums.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -8,7 +8,7 @@
 # Used by check-manifest-drift.sh to detect unauthorized changes.
 
 declare -gA ACFS_INTERNAL_CHECKSUMS=(
-  [scripts/lib/security.sh]="ba5f2cd39b67c65b92bb6ba08b1af3d972c6ebb619cf519d0b8df8d2967cb1a9"
+  [scripts/lib/security.sh]="0ae7abb60f16dbc3bc942d9f10784b64daee739b0c40515680f82a0ec2e7f5e4"
   [scripts/lib/agents.sh]="5a97482a9d6cb4aec990466577921ca442261483e87110ba68f2bbc88776d71b"
   [scripts/lib/update.sh]="a7958ad1b02fb1dc9fa67c1a15b364f683fd851d49c3a2d82547d22371e149a7"
   [scripts/lib/doctor.sh]="6818c233008db7d9fc169210fc5d76d4987a1e2734ef8c8fca2fba0d624720d8"

@@ -340,7 +340,6 @@ verify_checksum() {
     local expected_sha256="$2"
     local name="${3:-installer}"
     local fresh_tmp_file=""
-    local verify_tmp=""
 
     if ! enforce_https "$url"; then
         return 1
@@ -353,7 +352,7 @@ verify_checksum() {
         return 1
     }
     # Ensure cleanup on return without leaking a RETURN trap into callers.
-    trap 'rm -f "${tmp_file:-}" "${fresh_tmp_file:-}" "${verify_tmp:-}" 2>/dev/null || true; trap - RETURN' RETURN
+    trap 'rm -f "${tmp_file:-}" "${fresh_tmp_file:-}" 2>/dev/null || true; trap - RETURN' RETURN
 
     if ! acfs_download_to_file "$url" "$tmp_file" "$name"; then
         log_error "Security Error: Failed to fetch $name"
@@ -398,55 +397,9 @@ verify_checksum() {
             fi
         fi
 
-        # Trusted-tool auto-accept: for tools hosted under the same GitHub
-        # owner as ACFS itself (Dicklesworthstone), the checksum mismatch is
-        # almost certainly a stale checksums.yaml rather than a supply-chain
-        # attack (the same owner controls both ACFS and the tool repo).
-        #
-        # To proceed safely we:
-        #   1. Verify the URL belongs to the trusted owner
-        #   2. Re-download the installer to confirm consistency (guards
-        #      against transient corruption or MITM)
-        #   3. Accept the installer with a prominent warning
-        #
-        # External tools (ohmyzsh, rustup, bun, etc.) always hard-fail.
-        local trusted_owner="${ACFS_REPO_OWNER:-Dicklesworthstone}"
-        local effective_url="$url"
-        local effective_file="$tmp_file"
-        local effective_sha="$actual_sha256"
-        # Use the refreshed download if available and non-empty
-        if [[ -n "${fresh_tmp_file:-}" && -s "${fresh_tmp_file:-}" ]]; then
-            effective_file="$fresh_tmp_file"
-            effective_sha="${refreshed_actual_sha256:-$actual_sha256}"
-            effective_url="${refreshed_url:-$url}"
-        fi
-
-        if [[ "$effective_url" == *"githubusercontent.com/${trusted_owner}/"* ]] || \
-           [[ "$effective_url" == *"github.com/${trusted_owner}/"* ]]; then
-            # Consistency check: re-download and compare hash to rule out
-            # transient corruption or CDN split-brain.
-            verify_tmp="$(mktemp "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/acfs-trust-verify.XXXXXX" 2>/dev/null)" || verify_tmp=""
-            if [[ -n "$verify_tmp" ]]; then
-                local verify_sha=""
-                if acfs_download_to_file "$effective_url" "$verify_tmp" "${name}-verify" 2>/dev/null; then
-                    verify_sha="$(calculate_file_sha256 "$verify_tmp" 2>/dev/null)" || verify_sha=""
-                fi
-
-                if [[ -n "$verify_sha" && "$verify_sha" == "$effective_sha" ]]; then
-                    # Content is consistent across two downloads — accept it
-                    printf "${YELLOW}Warning: Trusted-tool auto-accept for %s${NC}\n" "$name" >&2
-                    printf "  Stale checksum: %s\n" "$expected_sha256" >&2
-                    printf "  Upstream hash:  %s\n" "$effective_sha" >&2
-                    printf "  URL: %s\n" "$effective_url" >&2
-                    printf "  The ACFS checksums.yaml is stale; accepting because %s owns both ACFS and this tool.\n" "$trusted_owner" >&2
-                    printf "  Checksums will be refreshed by the next checksum-monitor run.\n" >&2
-                    rm -f "$verify_tmp" 2>/dev/null
-                    cat "$effective_file"
-                    return $?
-                fi
-                rm -f "$verify_tmp" 2>/dev/null
-            fi
-        fi
+        # A trusted GitHub owner is not a substitute for checksum metadata.
+        # Consistent bytes across downloads may only prove CDN consistency; the
+        # installer still needs a matching checked-in or freshly loaded checksum.
 
         log_error "Security Error: Checksum mismatch for $name"
         printf "  Expected: %s\n" "$expected_sha256" >&2
